claimed_contribution: The paper proposes PIE, a holistic preference framework in which consumer choice is driven by needs determined by product physical attributes (P), individual characteristics (I), and external peer-group characteristics (E).
The paper introduces an incentive-aligned group-sourced preference measurement mechanism that captures real-time external feedback on E-driven needs during choice tasks.
The paper claims that PIE can improve predictive preference measurement relative to benchmark conjoint approaches and can be applied to product design by linking needs back to physical attributes.
context: The empirical settings span consumer products and services with different social visibility: sunglasses, bicycles, coffeemakers, and Italian restaurants. The studies are conducted with college-student participants in lab/online experimental tasks where peer input can be incorporated into choices.
design_and_data: The authors run three studies: Study 1 (sunglasses), Study 2 (bicycles and coffeemakers), and Study 3 (Italian restaurants). Studies 1 and 2 use between-subject designs comparing a group-sourced condition to a control condition; participants complete repeated choice sets, and in the group-sourced condition peers provide E-driven need ratings and profile recommendations. A one-week-later holdout task asks participants to choose from 20 options while accompanied by a friend; complete samples are 87 participants in Study 1 and 80 in Study 2. The analysis estimates hierarchical Bayes multinomial logit models (multiple P/I/E specifications), and Study 3 additionally uses 160 undergraduates to compare needs-based versus attribute-based tasks and map physical attributes to needs for holdout prediction.
main_findings: Across sunglasses, bicycles, and coffeemakers, incorporating E-driven needs improves holdout prediction versus benchmark models based only on P or on P plus I.

In most categories, using consultant-provided E evaluations in the group-sourced condition outperforms using only the focal participant's self-rated E evaluations (for example, sunglasses best model 53% vs. 39% holdout hit rate).

In the restaurant application, the needs-based condition slightly outperforms the physical-attribute condition in holdout prediction (26% vs. 21% and 20% vs. 18%), showing that needs-level modeling can be linked to product design through P-to-needs mapping.
one_line_summary: This paper introduces PIE and a group-sourced, incentive-aligned preference measurement method, and shows that explicitly incorporating external peer input improves prediction and supports needs-based product design.
relation_to_prior_work: extends traditional conjoint and Luo, Kannan, and Ratchford (2008) by explicitly modeling external social input (E) alongside product attributes (P) and individual factors (I) in a needs-to-choice structure. It also introduces a new data-collection mechanism to observe and use external feedback at the time of preference elicitation.
research_gap: Existing preference measurement methods, including conjoint and related subjective-criteria extensions, do not explicitly model or measure the influence of other people's opinions in preference formation. The paper identifies a practical measurement gap: a lack of feasible, incentive-compatible procedures to capture external feedback during the choice task.
research_question: Can individual preferences be measured more accurately when consumer needs are modeled as a function of P, I, and E rather than P (or P plus I) alone? Does an incentive-aligned group-sourced mechanism that captures external peer feedback improve out-of-sample prediction and provide actionable input for product design?
